describes read-only access.\n- **Sinks & Flows:** No sinks, standard flow.\n- **Anomalies:** None.\n- **Analysis & Conclusion:** Correct; it's a typical autogenerated SDK code.\n- **Scores:** Malware 0, Security Risk 0, Confidence 1, Obfuscated 0.\n\n**Verdict:** **Accurate.** No issues.\n\n---\n\n### **Report 2**\n- **Purpose & Sources:** Correct.\n- **Sinks & Flows:** No external data processing.\n- **Anomalies:** None.\n- **Analysis & Conclusion:** Correct; boilerplate code.\n- **Scores:** Malware 0, Security Risk 0, Confidence 1, Obfuscated 0.\n\n**Verdict:** **Accurate.** No issues.\n\n---\n\n### **Report 3**\n- **Purpose & Sources:** Correct.\n- **Sinks & Flows:** Accepts untrusted addresses for connection—standard in such factories.\n- **Anomalies:** Slightly higher security risk score (0.2) due to the potential misuse of the connect() method with malicious addresses.\n- **Analysis & Conclusion:** The analysis is sound; the only \"risk\" is the typical nature of connect() accepting arbitrary addresses, which is normal.\n\n**Scores:** Malware 0, Security Risk 0.2, Confidence 1, Obfuscated 0.\n\n**Justification for security risk score:**  \nSince the connect() method can be used with malicious addresses, but this is standard and not inherently malicious, a risk score of 0.2 seems reasonable, reflecting minimal risk due to potential misuse.\n\n**Verdict:** **Appropriate.** No malicious behavior detected.\n\n---\n\n### **Report 4**\n- Similar to previous; confirms standard patterns, no dynamic or malicious code.\n- Security risk score is 0, which is appropriate.\n\n**Verdict:** **Accurate.**\n\n---\n\n### **Report 5**\n- Same as above; standard autogenerated code.\n- No malicious behavior or obfuscation.\n- Security risk score 0 is correct.\n\n**Verdict:** **Accurate.**\n\n---\n\n### **Overall assessment:**\n\n- **Malware score:** All reports correctly identify no malicious activity.
